pattern_recognition: Data Structure Manipulation; this problem involves using one data structure (queues) to simulate another (stack), highlighting the versatility of data structures and the importance of understanding their properties.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Use one queue for storing elements and another queue for reversing the order of elements during pop operations.
approaches.algorithm: Push elements into the first queue. For pop operations, transfer all elements except the last one to the second queue, then pop the last element from the first queue and swap the queues.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Maintain one queue for push operations and use a single queue to reverse the order of elements only when needed for pop operations.
approaches.algorithm: For push, simply enqueue the element. For pop, if the queue has more than one element, transfer all but the last to a temporary queue, dequeue the last element, then swap the queues.

dry_run: Consider the operations: push(1), push(2), push(3), pop(), top(). Initially, the first queue is empty. After push(1), the first queue has [1]. After push(2), it has [1, 2]. After push(3), it has [1, 2, 3]. For pop(), transfer [1, 2] to the second queue, pop 3 from the first queue, and the second queue now has [2, 1]. The result of pop() is 3. For top(), the first queue is [2, 1], so the top is 2.
edge_cases: Empty input: operations on an empty stack should return errors. Single element: pushing and popping a single element should work correctly. Duplicates: pushing duplicate values should be handled properly. Large input: performance should be tested with a large number of operations. Overflow: ensure the implementation handles limits of queue capacity.
common_pitfalls: 1) Not handling the empty stack case properly when performing pop or top operations. 2) Forgetting to swap queues after pop operations, leading to incorrect order. 3) Inefficiently transferring elements back and forth between queues, causing performance issues.
